IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses an important problem in computational argumentation mining and provides concrete empirical findings showing that token-based sequence tagging (BiLSTM-CRF variants and multi-task tagging) outperforms framing AM as token-based dependency parsing and that joint MTL of subtasks improves end-to-end performance. By achieving new state-of-the-art results on the Persuasive Essays dataset, it will likely change model selection practices for researchers working on similar datasets and tasks. However, its impact is somewhat limited by evaluation on a single dataset (PE, 402 essays) and the narrow scope (no encoder-decoder exploration), so it is useful and influential but not transformative across NLP broadly.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well organized with a logical flow from motivation to methods and experiments, and the writing quality is reported as good. The main contributions and claims are stated early and clearly (e.g., comparisons between parsing- and tagging-based framings and MTL benefits). Notation and method descriptions appear consistent and well-defined given the thorough related-work coverage. A minor presentation shortcoming is the lack of reported hyperparameters and no mention of code availability, which slightly reduces completeness for readers reproducing the work.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against a comprehensive set of appropriate baselines (the original ILP model of Stab and Gurevych 2016, multiple dependency and neural parsers such as MST, Mate, Dyer et al., Kiperwasser & Goldberg, taggers like STagBLCC, and LSTM-ER). Comparisons use the same Persuasive Essays dataset and the established Component/Relation F1 metrics with level-α span matching, and the authors explicitly discuss when the parsing framing is inferior and when their tagging/MTL approach yields improvements (claiming new SOTA). Related work coverage is described as thorough; the only minor omission noted in the summary is lack of reported hyperparameters/code which could affect reproducibility but does not undermine the fairness or completeness of the empirical comparisons.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments against appropriate baselines (feature-based ILP, multiple dependency and neural parsers, STagBLCC, LSTM-ER) on the Persuasive Essays (PE) dataset, including component and relation F1 evaluations and reporting of level-α span matching; the paper further reports a new state-of-the-art on PE. There are no mathematical derivations to check. Methodologically it is mostly sound (relevant baselines and evaluation metrics), but important reproducibility and robustness details are missing in the summary (no hyperparameters reported, no mention of statistical significance tests or released code) and the evaluation is limited to a single dataset, so minor concerns remain about generality and rigor of experimental claims.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments compare a wide range of strong baselines (multiple neural and feature-based parsers, BiLSTM-CRF variants, BLCC tagger, LSTM-ER) on the PE dataset and claim improved end-to-end results, supporting the main empirical claims about parsing vs tagging and MTL benefits. However, no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported, hyperparameters are not provided, and encoder–decoder framing was left untested, limiting depth and reproducibility. Related work coverage is described as thorough and some negative results/limitations (poor performance of dependency framing) are discussed, but missing experimental details and analyses prevent a higher score.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper addresses computational argumentation mining, a well-established NLP task, using neural sequence tagging and parsing approaches and reports new state-of-the-art results on the widely used Persuasive Essays (PE) dataset, so it lies squarely within top NLP conference scope. It compares against appropriate baselines (feature-based ILP, multiple parsers, LSTM-ER, BLCC tagger), uses standard evaluation metrics (Component/Relation F1, level-α matching), and investigates multi-task learning and practical modeling choices that are of clear interest to the venue. The related-work coverage is thorough and the writing is good, indicating the paper is positioned correctly for a top-tier NLP audience.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper investigates framing argumentation mining as token-based dependency parsing versus token-based sequence tagging (including multi-task BiLSTM-CRF models) and reports a novel empirical finding that simpler tagging models outperform parsing approaches, yielding new state-of-the-art results on the Persuasive Essays dataset. However, the techniques used are existing neural architectures (BiLSTM-CRF variants, BLCC tagger, adapted LSTM-ER) and established multi-task learning rather than fundamentally new methods. Given the thorough comparisons to many parsers and the useful empirical insight (including the negative result about dependency-parsing framing), the contribution is moderately novel but essentially incremental.

main_claims: Framing argumentation mining as token-based dependency parsing yields subpar performance compared to tagging-based approaches.
Less complex local tagging models based on BiLSTMs (including BiLSTM-CRF variants) perform robustly and can capture long-range dependencies in end-to-end AM.
Jointly learning natural subtasks in a multi-task learning (MTL) setup improves end-to-end AM performance.
Their approach achieves new state-of-the-art results in end-to-end argumentation mining on the Persuasive Essays (PE) dataset of Stab and Gurevych (2016).
_summary.methodology: They investigate neural end-to-end approaches by framing AM as token-based dependency parsing and as token-based sequence tagging (including multi-task tagging), evaluating models such as BiLSTM-CRF variants, BLCC tagger, adapted LSTM-ER and several neural and feature-based parsers on the PE dataset.
datasets: Persuasive Essays (PE) dataset from Stab and Gurevych (2016) (402 essays, 80 test essays)
metrics: Level-α matching for span overlap (e.g., 100% exact, 50% approximate)
limitations: Framing AM as dependency parsing leads to subpar performance.
Framing AM as an encoder-decoder problem was not considered and is left as scope for future work.
